sections.title: The IPython Shell vs. a Python script

steps.label: The IPython Shell executes one Python command at a time and shows the
steps.note:
The IPython Shell executes one Python command at a time and shows the
result immediately — good for quick experiments, not for saved work.

sections.title: Types change behavior: + means different things for different types

steps.label: The same operator behaves differently depending on the operand types:
steps.note:
The same operator behaves differently depending on the operand types:
numeric + is arithmetic addition, string + is concatenation.
steps.code:

print('ab' + 'cd')  # 'abcd'

syntaxPointers.label: Exponentiation (**)
syntaxPointers.explanation: ** is power, not ^ (which is bitwise XOR in Python). It binds tighter than unary minus: -2**2 is -4.

syntaxPointers.label: isinstance() / type()
syntaxPointers.explanation: isinstance() respects inheritance (subclasses count); type() checks the exact class. Prefer isinstance for behavior checks.

syntaxPointers.label: print()
syntaxPointers.explanation: print() writes to stdout with a trailing newline. Control it with sep=, end=, and file= — e.g. end='' to suppress the newline.

quiz.question: Why does `type(day_of_week)` report `int` for `5` but `float` for `weight / height ** 2`?
options: `/` always produces a float in Python 3, even when both inputs are ints
Exponentiation converts everything to float
Any variable named with an underscore defaults to int
Division rounds, and rounded numbers are always floats
quiz.explanation: In Python 3 the `/` operator is true division and always yields a float, regardless of operand types. Use `//` for integer (floor) division.

quiz.question: Why does `'ab' + 'cd'` give `'abcd'` while `2 + 3` gives `5`?
options: Python guesses intent from the variable names
`+` is overloaded: concatenation for str, addition for numbers
Strings are secretly numbers under the hood
`+` always concatenates; `2 + 3` is a special case
quiz.explanation: The operator dispatches on operand type — `+` calls each type's own implementation. That's why `2 + '3'` raises TypeError rather than guessing.

sections.title: Behind the scenes: lists are references, not copies

steps.label: y = x does NOT create a new list — both names point at the same list
steps.note:
y = x does NOT create a new list — both names point at the same list
object, so mutating y through an index also changes what x sees.
steps.code:
x = ["a", "b", "c"]
y = x
y[1] = "z"
print(y)  # ['a', 'z', 'c']
print(x)  # ['a', 'z', 'c'] — x changed too, even though only y was assigned to

sections.title: Making an actual copy of a list

steps.label: list(x) or x[:] builds a genuinely separate list, so mutating the copy
steps.note:
list(x) or x[:] builds a genuinely separate list, so mutating the copy
leaves the original untouched.
steps.code:
x = ["a", "b", "c"]
y = list(x)   # or: y = x[:]

y[1] = "z"
print(x)  # ['a', 'b', 'c'] — unchanged, unlike the y = x case above

syntaxPointers.label: List literal
syntaxPointers.explanation: Square brackets build a mutable list. Assigning a list to a new name shares the same object — mutating one view mutates both.

syntaxPointers.label: Nested list / 2D structure
syntaxPointers.explanation: A list of lists is Python's basic 2D structure. Indexing is [row][col]; the inner lists are independent objects.

syntaxPointers.label: Negative indexing
syntaxPointers.explanation: [-1] counts from the end, so -1 is the last element. Going past the start (too-negative) raises IndexError, unlike slicing.

syntaxPointers.label: Slicing [start:stop]
syntaxPointers.explanation: start is inclusive, stop is exclusive. Omitting either end defaults to the beginning/end; slicing never raises for out-of-range bounds.

syntaxPointers.label: isinstance() / type()
syntaxPointers.explanation: isinstance() respects inheritance (subclasses count); type() checks the exact class. Prefer isinstance for behavior checks.

syntaxPointers.label: print()
syntaxPointers.explanation: print() writes to stdout with a trailing newline. Control it with sep=, end=, and file= — e.g. end='' to suppress the newline.

syntaxPointers.label: del statement
syntaxPointers.explanation: del removes a name binding or a container element in place. Deleting from a list shifts later indices down by one.

quiz.question: After `x = ["a","b","c"]; y = x; y[1] = "z"`, why does `x` also become `['a','z','c']`?
options: `y = x` copies the list, but index assignment writes through to the original
`y = x` binds y to the *same* list object, so both names see the one mutation
Lists are immutable, so Python silently rebuilt both
Strings inside lists are shared, so only the string changed
quiz.explanation: `y = x` copies the reference, not the list — both names point at one object. Use `list(x)` or `x[:]` for an independent copy.

topics.title: Functions, Methods & Packages
topics.description: Functions as reusable, pre-written pieces of code you call instead of re-implementing yourself; methods as functions that belong to a specific object's type; and packages as the mechanism for sharing and reusing someone else's functions and methods via import.

toc.label: Functions: reusable code that solves one task
round(): a function that takes more than one argument
Finding functions for a standard task
Methods: functions that belong to an object
Packages: why not ship everything in core Python

sections.title: Functions: reusable code that solves one task

steps.label: type() is already a function: a piece of reusable code that solves a
steps.note:

steps.label: Everything in Python is an object, and every object has methods
steps.note:

associated with it, depending on its type.
steps.code:

height = 1.73

steps.label: (untitled step)

steps.label: A method that exists on str doesn't exist on list, because the
steps.note:
A method that exists on str doesn't exist on list, because the
methods available depend on the object's type.

sections.title: Packages: why not ship everything in core Python

steps.label: Putting every possible function/method into the base Python
steps.note:
Putting every possible function/method into the base Python
distribution would make it a huge, messy, hard-to-maintain codebase
full of code most people never use. Instead, a package is a directory
of Python scripts (each script is a module) that specifies functions,
methods, and types for one purpose — e.g. NumPy, Matplotlib,
scikit-learn — installed only if you need it.

steps.label: Installed once from the terminal, e.g.:
steps.note:
Installed once from the terminal, e.g.:
python3 get-pip.py
pip3 install numpy
import numpy makes its contents available only via the numpy. prefix —
calling array() directly still fails.
steps.code:
import numpy
# array([1, 2, 3])          # NameError: name 'array' is not defined
print(numpy.array([1, 2, 3]))

steps.label: import numpy as np gives it a shorter alias.
steps.note: import numpy as np gives it a shorter alias.
steps.code:
import numpy as np
print(np.array([1, 2, 3]))

steps.label: from numpy import array pulls one name into the current namespace
steps.note:
from numpy import array pulls one name into the current namespace
directly, no prefix needed — but it's less clear later in the code
which package a bare name like array() came from.
steps.code:
from numpy import array
print(array([1, 2, 3]))

steps.label: Same script, less clear which package `array` came from:
steps.note: Same script, less clear which package `array` came from:
steps.code:
fam = ["liz", 1.73, "emma", 1.68,
       "mom", 1.71, "dad", 1.89]
fam_ext = fam + ["me", 1.79]
print(str(len(fam_ext)) + " elements in fam_ext")
np_fam = array(fam_ext)

steps.label: Same script, using the np. prefix makes the NumPy dependency explicit.
steps.note: Same script, using the np. prefix makes the NumPy dependency explicit.
steps.code:
fam_ext = fam + ["me", 1.79]
print(str(len(fam_ext)) + " elements in fam_ext")
np_fam = np.array(fam_ext)  # clearly using NumPy

syntaxPointers.label: List literal
syntaxPointers.explanation: Square brackets build a mutable list. Assigning a list to a new name shares the same object — mutating one view mutates both.

syntaxPointers.label: import module
syntaxPointers.explanation: import binds the module object; you reach its contents via the dotted prefix, keeping the origin of every name explicit.

syntaxPointers.label: import ... as (alias)
syntaxPointers.explanation: Aliasing (import numpy as np) shortens a long name while keeping the prefix, so calls still read as clearly coming from that package.

syntaxPointers.label: from ... import
syntaxPointers.explanation: from x import y pulls a name into the local namespace, so no prefix is needed — but it obscures which module y actually came from.

syntaxPointers.label: print()
syntaxPointers.explanation: print() writes to stdout with a trailing newline. Control it with sep=, end=, and file= — e.g. end='' to suppress the newline.

syntaxPointers.label: len()
syntaxPointers.explanation: len() is O(1) — containers store their size. It calls the object's __len__; objects without one raise TypeError.

syntaxPointers.label: String strip/replace
syntaxPointers.explanation: Strings are immutable, so these return a new string — the original is unchanged. Assign the result or it's lost.

syntaxPointers.label: String case methods
syntaxPointers.explanation: Case methods return a new string. capitalize() lowercases everything after the first letter; title() capitalizes each word.

syntaxPointers.label: sum() / min() / max()
syntaxPointers.explanation: These consume any iterable. min/max accept a key= like sorted; on an empty iterable they raise unless you pass default=.

syntaxPointers.label: help() / dir()
syntaxPointers.explanation: help() prints an object's docstring-based manual; dir() lists its attributes/methods. Both work live without leaving the shell.

syntaxPointers.label: list .append() / .extend()
syntaxPointers.explanation: append adds one item; extend adds each item of an iterable. append(list) nests a whole list as a single element — a common mix-up.

syntaxPointers.label: list .index() / .count()
syntaxPointers.explanation: .index() returns the first match's position and raises ValueError if absent; .count() returns how many times a value appears.

syntaxPointers.label: np.array()
syntaxPointers.explanation: np.array coerces to one dtype for the whole array — mixing int and float upcasts everything to float. That homogeneity is what makes it fast.

topics.title: Introducing DataFrames
topics.description: pandas is the go-to Python package for working with rectangular ("tidy") data: rows are observations, columns are variables. This chapter covers what a DataFrame is and how to explore one, how to sort and subset rows and columns, and how to add new columns computed from existing ones.

toc.label: Rectangular data: building the dogs DataFrame
Subsetting rows with multiple conditions and .isin()

steps.label: pandas is built on top of NumPy (fast numerical arrays) and Matplotlib
steps.note:
pandas is built on top of NumPy (fast numerical arrays) and Matplotlib
(plotting) — it adds labeled, tabular data structures on top of them.
It's one of the most popular Python packages for data manipulation and
feeds into both the Data Manipulation and Data Visualization skill tracks.
Course outline:
Chapter 1: DataFrames                      - sorting/subsetting, new columns
Chapter 2: Aggregating Data                 - summary stats, counting, groupby
Chapter 3: Slicing and Indexing Data         - slicing, indexes
Chapter 4: Creating and Visualizing Data     - plotting, missing data, I/O
pandas philosophy, quoting the Zen of Python:
"There should be one -- and preferably only one -- obvious way to do it."

sections.title: Rectangular data: building the dogs DataFrame

steps.label: Rectangular data: one row per observation (a dog), one column per
steps.note:
Rectangular data: one row per observation (a dog), one column per
variable (name, breed, color, height, weight, date of birth). A pandas
DataFrame is built here from a dictionary of lists — one key per column.
steps.code:
dogs = pd.DataFrame({
    "name": ["Bella", "Charlie", "Lucy", "Cooper", "Max", "Stella", "Bernie"],
    "breed": ["Labrador", "Poodle", "Chow Chow", "Schnauzer", "Labrador",
              "Chihuahua", "St. Bernard"],
    "color": ["Brown", "Black", "Brown", "Gray", "Black", "Tan", "White"],

syntaxPointers.label: List literal
syntaxPointers.explanation: Square brackets build a mutable list. Assigning a list to a new name shares the same object — mutating one view mutates both.

syntaxPointers.label: Exponentiation (**)
syntaxPointers.explanation: ** is power, not ^ (which is bitwise XOR in Python). It binds tighter than unary minus: -2**2 is -4.

syntaxPointers.label: Keyword argument at call site
syntaxPointers.explanation: name=value at a call passes by name, so order doesn't matter and intent is explicit. Keyword args must follow positional ones.

syntaxPointers.label: print()
syntaxPointers.explanation: print() writes to stdout with a trailing newline. Control it with sep=, end=, and file= — e.g. end='' to suppress the newline.

syntaxPointers.label: Double-bracket column selection
syntaxPointers.explanation: df[['a','b']] returns a DataFrame of several columns; the inner list is the column names. Single brackets df['a'] give a 1-D Series instead — the extra brackets are what keep it 2-D.

syntaxPointers.label: DataFrame aggregation (.mean/.sum/.agg)
syntaxPointers.explanation: These skip NaN by default, so a column's mean ignores missing values silently — check .isna().sum() if that matters.

quiz.question: Why does `df['a']` return a Series but `df[['a','b']]` return a DataFrame?
options: Double brackets are a tolerated syntax error
The inner list requests multiple columns, so the result stays 2-D (a DataFrame)
Single brackets always return the first column only
It depends on the column dtypes, not the brackets
quiz.explanation: `df['a']` selects one column → 1-D Series. `df[['a','b']]` passes a list of columns, keeping two dimensions → DataFrame. The extra brackets preserve 2-D shape.

topics.title: Aggregating Data
topics.description: Once a DataFrame is built and subset, the next step is to summarize it: single-number summary statistics, custom summaries via .agg(), running totals with cumulative statistics, counting (and avoiding double-counting) categorical data, and computing summaries per group with .groupby() and .pivot_table().

toc.label: The .agg() method for custom and multiple summaries
Grouping by multiple variables and multiple columns
Pivot tables: multiple statistics, two variables, fill_value and margins

steps.label: .agg() applies a custom summary function (taking a column, returning
steps.note:

steps.note:

print(dogs.groupby("color")["weight_kg"].mean())
# color
# Black    26.5
# Brown    24.0
# Gray     17.0
# Tan       2.0
# White    74.0
# Name: weight_kg, dtype: float64

steps.label: Passing a list of functions to .agg() applies each to every group.
steps.note: Passing a list of functions to .agg() applies each to every group.

print(dogs.groupby("color")["weight_kg"].agg([min, max, sum]))
#        min  max  sum
# color
# Black   24   29   53
# Brown   24   24   48
# Gray    17   17   17
# Tan      2    2    2
# White   74   74   74

steps.note: Passing a list of column names groups by every combination of them.

steps.note: Grouping by multiple columns and summarizing multiple columns at once.

steps.label: .pivot_table() is another way to write a groupby-and-summarize; the
steps.note:
.pivot_table() is another way to write a groupby-and-summarize; the
default aggregation function is the mean.

# Brown    24
# Gray     17
# Tan       2
# White    74

print(dogs.pivot_table(values="weight_kg", index="color"))

steps.note: aggfunc picks a different summary statistic.

# Brown       24.0
# Gray        17.0

steps.note: aggfunc can also take a list, to compute several statistics at once.

# Gray       17.0      17.0
# Tan         2.0       2.0
# White      74.0      74.0

sections.title: Pivot tables: multiple statistics, two variables, fill_value and margins

steps.label: Grouping by two variables is equivalent to a pivot table with one
steps.note:
Grouping by two variables is equivalent to a pivot table with one
variable as the index and the other spread across columns.
steps.code:
print(dogs.groupby(["color", "breed"])["weight_kg"].mean())

print(dogs.pivot_table(values="weight_kg", index="color", columns="breed"))
# breed  Chihuahua  Chow Chow  Labrador  Poodle  Schnauzer  St. Bernard

# Black        NaN        NaN      29.0    24.0        NaN          NaN
# Brown        NaN       24.0      24.0     NaN        NaN          NaN
# Gray         NaN        NaN       NaN     NaN       17.0          NaN
# Tan          2.0        NaN       NaN     NaN        NaN          NaN
# White        NaN        NaN       NaN     NaN        NaN         74.0

steps.label: fill_value replaces missing color/breed combinations with a given value.
steps.note: fill_value replaces missing color/breed combinations with a given value.
steps.code:
print(dogs.pivot_table(values="weight_kg", index="color", columns="breed",
                        fill_value=0))

# Tan            2          0         0       0          0            0

steps.note: margins=True adds an "All" row and column with the overall summaries.

print(dogs.pivot_table(values="weight_kg", index="color", columns="breed",

# color
# Black          0          0        29      24          0            0  26.500000
# Brown          0         24        24       0          0            0  24.000000
# Gray           0          0         0       0         17            0  17.000000
# Tan            2          0         0       0          0            0   2.000000
# White          0          0         0       0          0           74  74.000000

syntaxPointers.label: List literal
syntaxPointers.explanation: Square brackets build a mutable list. Assigning a list to a new name shares the same object — mutating one view mutates both.

syntaxPointers.label: Function definition (def)
syntaxPointers.explanation: def creates a function object at runtime. Default argument values are evaluated once at definition — mutable defaults are a classic trap.

syntaxPointers.label: return statement
syntaxPointers.explanation: A function with no return (or a bare return) yields None. Returning multiple comma-separated values actually returns one tuple.

syntaxPointers.label: Keyword argument at call site
syntaxPointers.explanation: name=value at a call passes by name, so order doesn't matter and intent is explicit. Keyword args must follow positional ones.

syntaxPointers.label: print()
syntaxPointers.explanation: print() writes to stdout with a trailing newline. Control it with sep=, end=, and file= — e.g. end='' to suppress the newline.

syntaxPointers.label: sum() / min() / max()
syntaxPointers.explanation: These consume any iterable. min/max accept a key= like sorted; on an empty iterable they raise unless you pass default=.

syntaxPointers.label: Double-bracket column selection
syntaxPointers.explanation: df[['a','b']] returns a DataFrame of several columns; the inner list is the column names. Single brackets df['a'] give a 1-D Series instead — the extra brackets are what keep it 2-D.

syntaxPointers.label: pd.read_csv()
syntaxPointers.explanation: read_csv infers dtypes column by column. Pass index_col= to promote a column to the index and parse_dates= to get real datetimes.

syntaxPointers.label: DataFrame .groupby()
syntaxPointers.explanation: groupby splits rows by key, then you apply an aggregation — nothing is computed until that aggregation runs (split-apply-combine).

syntaxPointers.label: DataFrame aggregation (.mean/.sum/.agg)
syntaxPointers.explanation: These skip NaN by default, so a column's mean ignores missing values silently — check .isna().sum() if that matters.

topics.title: Slicing and Indexing Data
topics.description: DataFrames don't have to use the default 0, 1, 2, ... row numbering: any column (or combination of columns) can become an explicit index. This chapter covers setting/removing indexes, subsetting and sorting by index (including multi-level indexes), slicing rows and columns with .loc and .iloc, and combining slicing with pivot tables.

toc.label: Setting and removing a column as the index
Indexes make subsetting simpler; duplicate index values
Slicing columns, and rows and columns together; subsetting by position with .iloc
.loc + slicing on pivot tables, and the axis argument

sections.title: Setting and removing a column as the index

steps.label: (untitled step)
steps.code:
print(dogs.columns)
# Index(['name', 'breed', 'color', 'height_cm', 'weight_kg'], dtype='object')
print(dogs.index)  # RangeIndex(start=0, stop=7, step=1)

steps.label: .set_index() moves a column out of the data and into the index.
steps.note: .set_index() moves a column out of the data and into the index.
steps.code:
dogs_ind = dogs.set_index("name")
print(dogs_ind)
#                breed  color  height_cm  weight_kg
# name
# Bella       Labrador  Brown         56         25
# Charlie       Poodle  Black         43         23
# Lucy       Chow Chow  Brown         46         22
# Cooper     Schnauzer   Gray         49         17
# Max         Labrador  Black         59         29
# Stella     Chihuahua    Tan         18          2
# Bernie   St. Bernard  White         77         74

steps.label: .reset_index() undoes that, moving the index back into a column.
steps.note: .reset_index() undoes that, moving the index back into a column.
steps.code:
print(dogs_ind.reset_index())
#       name        breed  color  height_cm  weight_kg
# 0    Bella     Labrador  Brown         56         25
# 1  Charlie       Poodle  Black         43         23
# 2     Lucy    Chow Chow  Brown         46         22
# 3   Cooper    Schnauzer   Gray         49         17
# 4      Max     Labrador  Black         59         29
# 5   Stella    Chihuahua    Tan         18          2
# 6   Bernie  St. Bernard  White         77         74

steps.label: drop=True discards the old index entirely instead of keeping it as a column.
steps.note: drop=True discards the old index entirely instead of keeping it as a column.
steps.code:
print(dogs_ind.reset_index(drop=True))
#         breed  color  height_cm  weight_kg
# 0    Labrador  Brown         56         25
# 1      Poodle  Black         43         23
# 2   Chow Chow  Brown         46         22
# 3   Schnauzer   Gray         49         17
# 4    Labrador  Black         59         29
# 5   Chihuahua    Tan         18          2
# 6 St. Bernard  White         77         74

sections.title: Indexes make subsetting simpler; duplicate index values

steps.label: Subsetting by name, the old way: a boolean condition with .isin().
steps.note: Subsetting by name, the old way: a boolean condition with .isin().
steps.code:
print(dogs[dogs["name"].isin(["Bella", "Stella"])])
#      name      breed  color  height_cm  weight_kg
# 0   Bella   Labrador  Brown         56         25
# 5  Stella  Chihuahua    Tan         18          2

steps.label: The same subset, using .loc[] with a list of index labels instead.
steps.note: The same subset, using .loc[] with a list of index labels instead.
steps.code:
print(dogs_ind.loc[["Bella", "Stella"]])
#             breed  color  height_cm  weight_kg
# name
# Bella    Labrador  Brown         56         25
# Stella  Chihuahua    Tan         18          2

steps.label: Index values don't need to be unique — here breed is used as the index,
steps.note:
Index values don't need to be unique — here breed is used as the index,
and "Labrador" appears twice.
steps.code:
dogs_ind2 = dogs.set_index("breed")
print(dogs_ind2)
#                 name  color  height_cm  weight_kg
# breed
# Labrador       Bella  Brown         56         25
# Poodle       Charlie  Black         43         23
# Chow Chow       Lucy  Brown         46         22
# Schnauzer     Cooper   Gray         49         17
# Labrador         Max  Black         59         29
# Chihuahua     Stella    Tan         18          2
# St. Bernard   Bernie  White         77         74

steps.label: .loc[] on a duplicated index label returns every matching row.
steps.note: .loc[] on a duplicated index label returns every matching row.
steps.code:
print(dogs_ind2.loc["Labrador"])
#            name  color  height_cm  weight_kg
# breed
# Labrador  Bella  Brown         56         25
# Labrador    Max  Black         59         29

steps.label: set_index() accepts a list of columns to build a multi-level index.
steps.note: set_index() accepts a list of columns to build a multi-level index.
steps.code:
dogs_ind3 = dogs.set_index(["breed", "color"])
print(dogs_ind3)
#                       name  height_cm  weight_kg
# breed       color
# Labrador    Brown    Bella         56         25
# Poodle      Black  Charlie         43         23
# Chow Chow   Brown     Lucy         46         22
# Schnauzer   Gray    Cooper         49         17
# Labrador    Black      Max         59         29
# Chihuahua   Tan     Stella         18          2
# St. Bernard White   Bernie         77         74

steps.label: Subset the outer index level with a plain list of labels.
steps.note: Subset the outer index level with a plain list of labels.
steps.code:
print(dogs_ind3.loc[["Labrador", "Chihuahua"]])
#                    name  height_cm  weight_kg
# breed     color
# Labrador  Brown   Bella         56         25
#           Black     Max         59         29
# Chihuahua Tan    Stella         18          2

steps.label: Subset combinations of outer + inner levels with a list of tuples.
steps.note: Subset combinations of outer + inner levels with a list of tuples.
steps.code:
print(dogs_ind3.loc[[("Labrador", "Brown"), ("Chihuahua", "Tan")]])
#                    name  height_cm  weight_kg
# breed     color
# Labrador  Brown   Bella         56         25
# Chihuahua Tan    Stella         18          2

steps.label: .sort_index() sorts by every index level, outer to inner, by default.
steps.note: .sort_index() sorts by every index level, outer to inner, by default.
steps.code:
print(dogs_ind3.sort_index())
#                       name  height_cm  weight_kg
# breed       color
# Chihuahua   Tan     Stella         18          2
# Chow Chow   Brown     Lucy         46         22
# Labrador    Black      Max         59         29
#             Brown    Bella         56         25
# Poodle      Black  Charlie         43         23
# Schnauzer   Gray    Cooper         49         17
# St. Bernard White   Bernie         77         74

steps.label: level and ascending give control over which levels sort which way.
steps.note: level and ascending give control over which levels sort which way.
steps.code:
print(dogs_ind3.sort_index(level=["color", "breed"], ascending=[True, False]))
#                       name  height_cm  weight_kg
# breed       color
# Poodle      Black  Charlie         43         23
# Labrador    Black      Max         59         29
#             Brown    Bella         56         25
# Chow Chow   Brown     Lucy         46         22
# Schnauzer   Gray    Cooper         49         17
# Chihuahua   Tan     Stella         18          2
# St. Bernard White   Bernie         77         74

steps.label: Indexes are a useful shortcut for subsetting, but they come at a cost:
steps.note:
Indexes are a useful shortcut for subsetting, but they come at a cost:
- Index values are just data, stored differently from the rest.
- Indexes violate "tidy data" principles (each variable a column).
- You need to learn two syntaxes: one for columns, one for the index.
Example of a "tidy" temperature dataset that keeps date/city/country as
ordinary columns rather than an index (avg_temp_c in degrees Celsius):
date      city           country       avg_temp_c
0 2000-01-01   Abidjan   Cote D'Ivoire            27.293
1 2000-02-01   Abidjan   Cote D'Ivoire            27.685
2 2000-03-01   Abidjan   Cote D'Ivoire            29.061
3 2000-04-01   Abidjan   Cote D'Ivoire            28.162
4 2000-05-01   Abidjan   Cote D'Ivoire            27.547

steps.label: Ordinary list slicing works on position, [start:end], end exclusive.
steps.note: Ordinary list slicing works on position, [start:end], end exclusive.
steps.code:
breeds = ["Labrador", "Poodle", "Chow Chow", "Schnauzer", "Labrador",
          "Chihuahua", "St. Bernard"]
print(breeds)
# ['Labrador', 'Poodle', 'Chow Chow', 'Schnauzer', 'Labrador', 'Chihuahua', 'St. Bernard']

print(breeds[2:5])  # ['Chow Chow', 'Schnauzer', 'Labrador']
print(breeds[:3])   # ['Labrador', 'Poodle', 'Chow Chow']
print(breeds[:])    # the whole list, unchanged

steps.label: The index must be sorted first, or slicing with .loc gives wrong/empty
steps.note:
The index must be sorted first, or slicing with .loc gives wrong/empty
results.
steps.code:
dogs_srt = dogs.set_index(["breed", "color"]).sort_index()
print(dogs_srt)
#                       name  height_cm  weight_kg
# breed       color
# Chihuahua   Tan     Stella         18          2
# Chow Chow   Brown     Lucy         46         22
# Labrador    Black      Max         59         29
#             Brown    Bella         56         25
# Poodle      Black  Charlie         43         23
# Schnauzer   Gray    Cooper         49         17
# St. Bernard White   Bernie         77         74

steps.label: Slicing the outer index level works like slicing a sorted list; the
steps.note:
Slicing the outer index level works like slicing a sorted list; the
final value ("Poodle") IS included, unlike list slicing.
steps.code:
print(dogs_srt.loc["Chow Chow":"Poodle"])
#                     name  height_cm  weight_kg
# breed     color
# Chow Chow Brown     Lucy         46         22
# Labrador  Black      Max         59         29
#           Brown    Bella         56         25
# Poodle    Black  Charlie         43         23

steps.label: Slicing the INNER level directly (ignoring the outer level) silently
steps.note:
Slicing the INNER level directly (ignoring the outer level) silently
gives an empty result — it doesn't do what you'd expect.
steps.code:
print(dogs_srt.loc["Tan":"Grey"])
# Empty DataFrame
# Columns: [name, height_cm, weight_kg]
# Index: []

steps.label: The correct way to slice inner levels: pass a tuple of
steps.note:
The correct way to slice inner levels: pass a tuple of
(outer, inner) labels for both the start and the end.
steps.code:
print(dogs_srt.loc[("Labrador", "Brown"):("Schnauzer", "Gray")])
#                     name  height_cm  weight_kg
# breed     color
# Labrador  Brown    Bella         56         25
# Poodle    Black  Charlie         43         23
# Schnauzer Gray    Cooper         49         17

steps.label: Setting a date column as the index (and sorting it) allows slicing a
steps.note:
Setting a date column as the index (and sorting it) allows slicing a
date range with .loc, the same way as slicing any other sorted index.
steps.code:
dogs = dogs.assign(date_of_birth=["2013-07-01", "2016-09-16", "2014-08-25",
                                   "2011-12-11", "2017-01-20", "2015-04-20",
                                   "2018-02-27"])
dogs = dogs.set_index("date_of_birth").sort_index()
print(dogs)
#                   name        breed  color  height_cm  weight_kg
# date_of_birth
# 2011-12-11      Cooper    Schnauzer   Gray         49         17
# 2013-07-01       Bella     Labrador  Brown         56         25
# 2014-08-25        Lucy    Chow Chow  Brown         46         22
# 2015-04-20      Stella    Chihuahua    Tan         18          2
# 2016-09-16     Charlie       Poodle  Black         43         23
# 2017-01-20         Max     Labrador  Black         59         29
# 2018-02-27      Bernie  St. Bernard  White         77         74

steps.label: Get dogs with date_of_birth between 2014-08-25 and 2016-09-16 (inclusive).
steps.note: Get dogs with date_of_birth between 2014-08-25 and 2016-09-16 (inclusive).
steps.code:
print(dogs.loc["2014-08-25":"2016-09-16"])
#                   name      breed  color  height_cm  weight_kg
# date_of_birth
# 2014-08-25        Lucy  Chow Chow  Brown         46         22
# 2015-04-20      Stella  Chihuahua    Tan         18          2
# 2016-09-16     Charlie     Poodle  Black         43         23

steps.label: A partial date ("2014", "2016") slices by year, matching every date
steps.note:
A partial date ("2014", "2016") slices by year, matching every date
that falls inside it: everything from 2014-01-01 to 2016-12-31.
steps.code:
print(dogs.loc["2014":"2016"])
#                  name      breed  color  height_cm  weight_kg
# date_of_birth
# 2014-08-25       Lucy  Chow Chow  Brown         46         22
# 2015-04-20     Stella  Chihuahua    Tan         18          2
# 2016-09-16    Charlie     Poodle  Black         43         23

sections.title: Slicing columns, and rows and columns together; subsetting by position with .iloc

steps.label: .loc[rows, columns] — a column slice works the same way as a row slice.
steps.note: .loc[rows, columns] — a column slice works the same way as a row slice.
steps.code:
print(dogs_srt.loc[:, "name":"height_cm"])
#                       name  height_cm
# breed       color
# Chihuahua   Tan     Stella         18
# Chow Chow   Brown     Lucy         46
# Labrador    Black      Max         59
#             Brown    Bella         56
# Poodle      Black  Charlie         43
# Schnauzer   Gray    Cooper         49
# St. Bernard White   Bernie         77

steps.label: Rows and columns can be sliced at the same time, in a single .loc call.
steps.note: Rows and columns can be sliced at the same time, in a single .loc call.
steps.code:
print(dogs_srt.loc[("Labrador", "Brown"):("Schnauzer", "Gray"),
                    "name":"height_cm"])
#                     name  height_cm
# breed     color
# Labrador  Brown    Bella         56
# Poodle    Black  Charlie         43
# Schnauzer Gray    Cooper         49

steps.label: .iloc[] slices by row/column position instead of by label.
steps.note: .iloc[] slices by row/column position instead of by label.
steps.code:
print(dogs.iloc[2:5, 1:4])
#        breed  color  height_cm
# 2  Chow Chow  Brown         46
# 3  Schnauzer   Gray         49
# 4   Labrador  Black         59

steps.note:

steps.code:

syntaxPointers.label: List literal
syntaxPointers.explanation: Square brackets build a mutable list. Assigning a list to a new name shares the same object — mutating one view mutates both.

syntaxPointers.label: Slicing [start:stop]
syntaxPointers.explanation: start is inclusive, stop is exclusive. Omitting either end defaults to the beginning/end; slicing never raises for out-of-range bounds.

syntaxPointers.label: Keyword argument at call site
syntaxPointers.explanation: name=value at a call passes by name, so order doesn't matter and intent is explicit. Keyword args must follow positional ones.

syntaxPointers.label: print()
syntaxPointers.explanation: print() writes to stdout with a trailing newline. Control it with sep=, end=, and file= — e.g. end='' to suppress the newline.

syntaxPointers.label: Double-bracket column selection
syntaxPointers.explanation: df[['a','b']] returns a DataFrame of several columns; the inner list is the column names. Single brackets df['a'] give a 1-D Series instead — the extra brackets are what keep it 2-D.

syntaxPointers.label: DataFrame .loc / .iloc
syntaxPointers.explanation: .loc selects by label (end-inclusive!), .iloc by integer position (end-exclusive). Mixing them up is the classic pandas indexing bug.

syntaxPointers.label: pd.read_csv()
syntaxPointers.explanation: read_csv infers dtypes column by column. Pass index_col= to promote a column to the index and parse_dates= to get real datetimes.

syntaxPointers.label: DataFrame aggregation (.mean/.sum/.agg)
syntaxPointers.explanation: These skip NaN by default, so a column's mean ignores missing values silently — check .isna().sum() if that matters.

syntaxPointers.label: Method chaining
syntaxPointers.explanation: Chaining a.b().c() reads left-to-right as a pipeline. Each call returns an object the next call operates on; a broken link mid-chain is harder to debug.

quiz.question: Why can `.loc['A':'C']` include row 'C' while `.iloc[0:3]` excludes position 3?
options: .loc is label-based and inclusive of the end; .iloc is position-based and exclusive
It's a compatibility bug pandas keeps
.loc counts from 1, .iloc from 0
Both are exclusive; the example is wrong
quiz.explanation: Label slicing (`.loc`) includes the stop label — you named it, so you want it. Positional slicing (`.iloc`) follows Python's half-open ranges and excludes the stop.

sections.title: Creating DataFrames: list of dictionaries vs. dictionary of lists

steps.label: A plain Python dictionary maps keys to values, looked up by key.
steps.note: A plain Python dictionary maps keys to values, looked up by key.

steps.label: A DataFrame can be built two ways:
steps.note:
A DataFrame can be built two ways:
- from a list of dictionaries: constructed row by row
- from a dictionary of lists: constructed column by column
List of dictionaries: each dict is one row, keys become column names.
steps.code:
list_of_dicts = [
    {"name": "Ginger", "breed": "Dachshund", "height_cm": 22,
     "weight_kg": 10, "date_of_birth": "2019-03-14"},

    {"name": "Scout", "breed": "Dalmatian", "height_cm": 59,
     "weight_kg": 25, "date_of_birth": "2019-05-09"},
]
new_dogs = pd.DataFrame(list_of_dicts)
print(new_dogs)
#      name      breed  height_cm  weight_kg date_of_birth
# 0  Ginger  Dachshund         22         10    2019-03-14
# 1   Scout  Dalmatian         59         25    2019-05-09

steps.label: Dictionary of lists: each key is a column name, each value a list of
steps.note:
Dictionary of lists: each key is a column name, each value a list of
that column's values (same order across all the lists).
steps.code:
dict_of_lists = {
    "name": ["Ginger", "Scout"],
    "breed": ["Dachshund", "Dalmatian"],
    "height_cm": [22, 59],
    "weight_kg": [10, 25],
    "date_of_birth": ["2019-03-14", "2019-05-09"],
}
new_dogs = pd.DataFrame(dict_of_lists)
print(new_dogs)
#      name      breed  height_cm  weight_kg date_of_birth
# 0  Ginger  Dachshund         22         10    2019-03-14
# 1   Scout  Dalmatian         59         25    2019-05-09

steps.label: CSV = comma-separated values, designed for exactly this kind of
steps.note:
CSV = comma-separated values, designed for exactly this kind of
rectangular data; most database/spreadsheet software can read and
write it. new_dogs.csv would look like:
name,breed,height_cm,weight_kg,d_o_b
Ginger,Dachshund,22,10,2019-03-14
Scout,Dalmatian,59,25,2019-05-09
pd.read_csv() turns a CSV file straight into a DataFrame.

new_dogs = pd.read_csv("new_dogs.csv")

# 1   Scout  Dalmatian         59         25    2019-05-09

steps.note: A DataFrame loaded from CSV can be manipulated exactly like any other.

syntaxPointers.label: List literal
syntaxPointers.explanation: Square brackets build a mutable list. Assigning a list to a new name shares the same object — mutating one view mutates both.

syntaxPointers.label: Exponentiation (**)
syntaxPointers.explanation: ** is power, not ^ (which is bitwise XOR in Python). It binds tighter than unary minus: -2**2 is -4.

syntaxPointers.label: Keyword argument at call site
syntaxPointers.explanation: name=value at a call passes by name, so order doesn't matter and intent is explicit. Keyword args must follow positional ones.

syntaxPointers.label: print()
syntaxPointers.explanation: print() writes to stdout with a trailing newline. Control it with sep=, end=, and file= — e.g. end='' to suppress the newline.

syntaxPointers.label: sum() / min() / max()
syntaxPointers.explanation: These consume any iterable. min/max accept a key= like sorted; on an empty iterable they raise unless you pass default=.

syntaxPointers.label: any() / all()
syntaxPointers.explanation: any()/all() short-circuit on the first decisive item. all() on an empty iterable is True; any() on empty is False.

syntaxPointers.label: pd.read_csv()
syntaxPointers.explanation: read_csv infers dtypes column by column. Pass index_col= to promote a column to the index and parse_dates= to get real datetimes.

syntaxPointers.label: DataFrame .groupby()
syntaxPointers.explanation: groupby splits rows by key, then you apply an aggregation — nothing is computed until that aggregation runs (split-apply-combine).

syntaxPointers.label: DataFrame aggregation (.mean/.sum/.agg)
syntaxPointers.explanation: These skip NaN by default, so a column's mean ignores missing values silently — check .isna().sum() if that matters.

syntaxPointers.label: Method chaining
syntaxPointers.explanation: Chaining a.b().c() reads left-to-right as a pipeline. Each call returns an object the next call operates on; a broken link mid-chain is harder to debug.

quiz.question: Why is a vectorized column operation preferred over looping rows with `.iterrows()`?
options: iterrows() can only read, never compute
iterrows() rebuilds a Series per row and drops dtypes — slow and lossy
apply() runs on the GPU
There's no real difference; it's style
quiz.explanation: `.iterrows()` yields a fresh Series each iteration and coerces mixed dtypes to object — slow and type-lossy. Vectorized operations run in C over the whole column at once.

quiz.question: Why is `fig, ax = plt.subplots()` written as a two-name assignment?
options: subplots() returns a tuple (Figure, Axes) that gets unpacked into two names
It creates two independent figures at once
fig and ax are keyword arguments set to defaults
The comma makes matplotlib render faster
quiz.explanation: `plt.subplots()` returns a `(Figure, Axes)` tuple; the comma unpacks it. The Figure is the canvas, the Axes is the plot you draw on.

quiz.question: Several `ax.plot(...)` calls precede a single `plt.show()`. Why do they share one figure?
options: show() merges the most recent plots automatically
Each plot() draws onto the same Axes; show() just renders the current state
matplotlib caches and deduplicates plots
Only the last plot() actually runs
quiz.explanation: Plotting calls mutate the Axes; nothing displays until `plt.show()` renders the accumulated state — so you build a figure with several calls, then show once.

steps.label: ax.twinx() creates a second Axes sharing the same x-axis but with its

sections.title: A reusable function for plotting a time series

steps.label: Wrapping the repeated plot/xlabel/ylabel/tick_params calls in a
steps.note:
Wrapping the repeated plot/xlabel/ylabel/tick_params calls in a
function avoids retyping them for every variable.
steps.code:
def plot_timeseries(axes, x, y, color, xlabel, ylabel):
    axes.plot(x, y, color=color)
    axes.set_xlabel(xlabel)
    axes.set_ylabel(ylabel, color=color)
    axes.tick_params('y', colors=color)

syntaxPointers.label: List literal
syntaxPointers.explanation: Square brackets build a mutable list. Assigning a list to a new name shares the same object — mutating one view mutates both.

syntaxPointers.label: Tuple packing / unpacking
syntaxPointers.explanation: a, b = ... unpacks an iterable into multiple names by position. The right side must have exactly as many items as names.

syntaxPointers.label: Function definition (def)
syntaxPointers.explanation: def creates a function object at runtime. Default argument values are evaluated once at definition — mutable defaults are a classic trap.
syntaxPointers.example: def plot_timeseries(axes, x, y, color, xlabel, ylabel):

syntaxPointers.label: Keyword argument at call site
syntaxPointers.explanation: name=value at a call passes by name, so order doesn't matter and intent is explicit. Keyword args must follow positional ones.

syntaxPointers.label: import module
syntaxPointers.explanation: import binds the module object; you reach its contents via the dotted prefix, keeping the origin of every name explicit.

syntaxPointers.label: import ... as (alias)
syntaxPointers.explanation: Aliasing (import numpy as np) shortens a long name while keeping the prefix, so calls still read as clearly coming from that package.

syntaxPointers.label: print()
syntaxPointers.explanation: print() writes to stdout with a trailing newline. Control it with sep=, end=, and file= — e.g. end='' to suppress the newline.

syntaxPointers.label: pd.read_csv()
syntaxPointers.explanation: read_csv infers dtypes column by column. Pass index_col= to promote a column to the index and parse_dates= to get real datetimes.

syntaxPointers.label: List literal
syntaxPointers.explanation: Square brackets build a mutable list. Assigning a list to a new name shares the same object — mutating one view mutates both.

syntaxPointers.label: Tuple packing / unpacking
syntaxPointers.explanation: a, b = ... unpacks an iterable into multiple names by position. The right side must have exactly as many items as names.

syntaxPointers.label: Keyword argument at call site
syntaxPointers.explanation: name=value at a call passes by name, so order doesn't matter and intent is explicit. Keyword args must follow positional ones.

syntaxPointers.label: import module
syntaxPointers.explanation: import binds the module object; you reach its contents via the dotted prefix, keeping the origin of every name explicit.

syntaxPointers.label: import ... as (alias)
syntaxPointers.explanation: Aliasing (import numpy as np) shortens a long name while keeping the prefix, so calls still read as clearly coming from that package.

syntaxPointers.label: pd.read_csv()
syntaxPointers.explanation: read_csv infers dtypes column by column. Pass index_col= to promote a column to the index and parse_dates= to get real datetimes.

syntaxPointers.label: DataFrame aggregation (.mean/.sum/.agg)
syntaxPointers.explanation: These skip NaN by default, so a column's mean ignores missing values silently — check .isna().sum() if that matters.

Ends with pointers to where to explore Matplotlib further.

steps.label: - Dark backgrounds are usually less visible (e.g. when projected).

- If color matters, prefer colorblind-friendly styles such as

- If the figure may be printed, prefer a style that uses less ink.
- If it will be printed in black-and-white, use the "grayscale" style.

sections.title: Automatically building a bar chart with a loop

steps.label: Looping over sports and filtering the DataFrame each time builds one
steps.note:
Looping over sports and filtering the DataFrame each time builds one
bar (with error bars) per sport, without hardcoding any sport name.
steps.code:
fig, ax = plt.subplots()

for sport in sports:
    sport_df = summer_2016_medals[summer_2016_medals["Sport"] == sport]
    ax.bar(sport, sport_df["Height"].mean(),
           yerr=sport_df["Height"].std())

ax.set_ylabel("Height (cm)")
ax.set_xticklabels(sports, rotation=90)
plt.show()

syntaxPointers.explanation: a, b = ... unpacks an iterable into multiple names by position. The right side must have exactly as many items as names.

syntaxPointers.label: for loop
syntaxPointers.explanation: Python's for iterates over items directly, not indices. To get indices too, wrap the iterable in enumerate().

syntaxPointers.explanation: name=value at a call passes by name, so order doesn't matter and intent is explicit. Keyword args must follow positional ones.

syntaxPointers.label: import module
syntaxPointers.explanation: import binds the module object; you reach its contents via the dotted prefix, keeping the origin of every name explicit.

syntaxPointers.label: import ... as (alias)
syntaxPointers.explanation: Aliasing (import numpy as np) shortens a long name while keeping the prefix, so calls still read as clearly coming from that package.

syntaxPointers.label: print()
syntaxPointers.explanation: print() writes to stdout with a trailing newline. Control it with sep=, end=, and file= — e.g. end='' to suppress the newline.

syntaxPointers.label: DataFrame aggregation (.mean/.sum/.agg)
syntaxPointers.explanation: These skip NaN by default, so a column's mean ignores missing values silently — check .isna().sum() if that matters.

syntaxPointers.label: Keyword argument at call site
syntaxPointers.explanation: name=value at a call passes by name, so order doesn't matter and intent is explicit. Keyword args must follow positional ones.

syntaxPointers.label: print()
syntaxPointers.explanation: print() writes to stdout with a trailing newline. Control it with sep=, end=, and file= — e.g. end='' to suppress the newline.

syntaxPointers.label: sum() / min() / max()
syntaxPointers.explanation: These consume any iterable. min/max accept a key= like sorted; on an empty iterable they raise unless you pass default=.

syntaxPointers.label: Double-bracket column selection
syntaxPointers.explanation: df[['a','b']] returns a DataFrame of several columns; the inner list is the column names. Single brackets df['a'] give a 1-D Series instead — the extra brackets are what keep it 2-D.

syntaxPointers.label: pd.read_csv()
syntaxPointers.explanation: read_csv infers dtypes column by column. Pass index_col= to promote a column to the index and parse_dates= to get real datetimes.

syntaxPointers.label: DataFrame .groupby()
syntaxPointers.explanation: groupby splits rows by key, then you apply an aggregation — nothing is computed until that aggregation runs (split-apply-combine).

syntaxPointers.label: DataFrame aggregation (.mean/.sum/.agg)
syntaxPointers.explanation: These skip NaN by default, so a column's mean ignores missing values silently — check .isna().sum() if that matters.

syntaxPointers.label: Method chaining
syntaxPointers.explanation: Chaining a.b().c() reads left-to-right as a pipeline. Each call returns an object the next call operates on; a broken link mid-chain is harder to debug.

quiz.question: A column's `.mean()` returns a number even though the column has missing values. Why no error or NaN?
options: pandas fills missing values with 0 before averaging
pandas aggregations skip NaN by default (skipna=True)
The missing values were dropped on import
mean() only reads the first non-null value
quiz.explanation: Most pandas reductions default to skipna=True, quietly ignoring NaN. Convenient, but it can hide data-quality issues — check `.isna().sum()` first.

topics.title: Missing Data, Categorical & Numeric Data, and Outliers
topics.description: Cleaning up a real-world "data professionals' salaries" dataset: finding and addressing missing values (drop vs. impute), turning messy free-text job titles into a small set of categories, converting a string-typed numeric column into a usable number, and detecting/handling outliers with the interquartile range (IQR).

toc.label: Dropping missing values above a threshold
Extracting value from categories with str.contains()
Creating a categorical column with np.select()
Adding summary statistics into a DataFrame with .transform()
Using descriptive statistics and the IQR

steps.label: Missing data skews distributions and can misrepresent whole subgroups
steps.note:
Missing data skews distributions and can misrepresent whole subgroups
(e.g. dropping the oldest/tallest respondents), leading to wrong
conclusions. .isna().sum() counts nulls per column.
steps.code:
print(salaries.isna().sum())
# Working_Year            12
# Designation             27
# Experience              33
# Employment_Status       31
# Employee_Location       28
# Company_Size            40
# Remote_Working_Ratio    24
# Salary_USD              60
# dtype: int64

steps.label: Three common strategies, depending on how much is missing and why:
steps.note:
Three common strategies, depending on how much is missing and why:
- Drop missing values          (rule of thumb: 5% or less of total values)
- Impute mean, median, or mode  (depends on the distribution/context)
- Impute by sub-group           (e.g. different experience levels have
different median salaries)

sections.title: Dropping missing values above a threshold

steps.label: A 5%-of-rows threshold decides which sparsely-missing columns are safe
steps.note:
A 5%-of-rows threshold decides which sparsely-missing columns are safe
to drop rows for.
steps.code:
threshold = len(salaries) * 0.05
print(threshold)  # 30

steps.label: Columns at or below the threshold of missing values are dropped by row.
steps.note: Columns at or below the threshold of missing values are dropped by row.
steps.code:
cols_to_drop = salaries.columns[salaries.isna().sum() <= threshold]
print(cols_to_drop)
# Index(['Working_Year', 'Designation', 'Employee_Location',
#        'Remote_Working_Ratio'],
#       dtype='object')

salaries.dropna(subset=cols_to_drop, inplace=True)

steps.label: Columns with more missing values (above the threshold) are imputed
steps.note:
Columns with more missing values (above the threshold) are imputed
instead of dropped.
steps.code:
cols_with_missing_values = salaries.columns[salaries.isna().sum() > 0]
print(cols_with_missing_values)
# Index(['Experience', 'Employment_Status', 'Company_Size', 'Salary_USD'],
#       dtype='object')

steps.label: Impute the mode for every missing column except the last one
steps.note:
Impute the mode for every missing column except the last one
(Salary_USD is numeric and gets a more tailored treatment next).
NOTE: .fillna() here returns a new Series that isn't captured — to make
this actually persist you'd need salaries[col] = salaries[col].fillna(...)
or fillna(..., inplace=True).
steps.code:
for col in cols_with_missing_values[:-1]:
    salaries[col].fillna(salaries[col].mode()[0])

steps.label: Checking what's left to impute: only the numeric Salary_USD column.
steps.note: Checking what's left to impute: only the numeric Salary_USD column.
steps.code:
print(salaries.isna().sum())
# Working_Year             0
# Designation              0
# Experience               0
# Employment_Status        0
# Employee_Location        0
# Company_Size             0
# Remote_Working_Ratio     0
# Salary_USD              41

steps.label: Different experience levels have different typical salaries, so impute
steps.note:
Different experience levels have different typical salaries, so impute
the median salary per sub-group (Experience) rather than one global median.

steps.label: .map() looks up each row's Experience in salaries_dict; fillna() uses that
steps.note:
.map() looks up each row's Experience in salaries_dict; fillna() uses that
as the fill value for missing Salary_USD entries.
steps.code: salaries["Salary_USD"] = salaries["Salary_USD"].fillna(salaries["Experience"].map(salaries_dict))

steps.label: No more missing values!
steps.note: No more missing values!
steps.code:
print(salaries.isna().sum())
# Working_Year            0
# Designation             0
# Experience              0
# Employment_Status       0
# Employee_Location       0
# Company_Size            0
# Remote_Working_Ratio    0
# Salary_USD              0
# dtype: int64

sections.title: Extracting value from categories with str.contains()

steps.label: The current free-text format limits our ability to generate insights.
steps.note:
The current free-text format limits our ability to generate insights.
str.contains() searches a column for a specific string.
steps.code:
print(salaries["Designation"].str.contains("Scientist"))
# 0       True
# 1       True
# 2      False
# 3      False
#        ...
# 604    False
# 605    False
# 606     True
# Name: Designation, Length: 607, dtype: bool

steps.label: | (or) matches either phrase, e.g. titles mentioning Machine Learning or AI.
steps.note: | (or) matches either phrase, e.g. titles mentioning Machine Learning or AI.
steps.code:
print(salaries["Designation"].str.contains("Machine Learning|AI"))
# 0      False
# 1       True
# 2      False
# 3      False
#        ...
# 604    False
# 605    False
# 606     True
# Name: Designation, Length: 607, dtype: bool

steps.label: ^ anchors the match to the start of the string, e.g. titles starting with Data.
steps.note: ^ anchors the match to the start of the string, e.g. titles starting with Data.
steps.code:
print(salaries["Designation"].str.contains("^Data"))
# 0       True
# 1      False
# 2      False
# 3      False
#        ...
# 604     True
# 605     True
# 606    False
# Name: Designation, Length: 607, dtype: bool

steps.label: The target set of job categories to bucket every title into.
steps.note: The target set of job categories to bucket every title into.
steps.code:
job_categories = ["Data Science", "Data Analytics",
                   "Data Engineering", "Machine Learning",
                   "Managerial", "Consultant"]

steps.label: One regex pattern of matching phrases per category.
steps.note: One regex pattern of matching phrases per category.
steps.code:
data_science = "Data Scientist|NLP"
data_analyst = "Analyst|Analytics"
data_engineer = "Data Engineer|ETL|Architect|Infrastructure"
ml_engineer = "Machine Learning|ML|Big Data|AI"
manager = "Manager|Head|Director|Lead|Principal|Staff"
consultant = "Consultant|Freelance"

steps.label: One boolean condition per category, in the same order as job_categories.
steps.note: One boolean condition per category, in the same order as job_categories.
steps.code:
conditions = [
    (salaries["Designation"].str.contains(data_science)),
    (salaries["Designation"].str.contains(data_analyst)),
    (salaries["Designation"].str.contains(data_engineer)),
    (salaries["Designation"].str.contains(ml_engineer)),
    (salaries["Designation"].str.contains(manager)),
    (salaries["Designation"].str.contains(consultant))
]

steps.label: np.select() picks the matching job_categories label for the first True
steps.note:

steps.code:

                                      job_categories,

sections.title: Adding summary statistics into a DataFrame with .transform()

steps.label: A plain groupby-mean summarizes each Company_Size in its own small table.
steps.note: A plain groupby-mean summarizes each Company_Size in its own small table.
steps.code:
print(salaries.groupby("Company_Size")["Salary_USD"].mean())
# Company_Size
# L    111934.432174
# M    110706.628527
# S     69880.980179
# Name: Salary_USD, dtype: float64

steps.label: .transform() instead broadcasts the group statistic back onto every row,
steps.note:
.transform() instead broadcasts the group statistic back onto every row,
so it can be stored as a new column on the original DataFrame.
steps.code:
salaries["std_dev"] = salaries.groupby("Experience")["Salary_USD"].transform(lambda x: x.std())
print(salaries[["Experience", "std_dev"]].value_counts())
# Experience         std_dev
# SE                 52995.385395        257
# MI                 63217.397343        197
# EN                 43367.256303         83
# EX                 86426.611619         24

steps.label: Works the same way for any group/column/statistic combination.
steps.note: Works the same way for any group/column/statistic combination.
steps.code:
salaries["median_by_comp_size"] = salaries.groupby("Company_Size") \
                                        ["Salary_USD"].transform(lambda x: x.median())
print(salaries[["Company_Size", "median_by_comp_size"]].head())
#   Company_Size  median_by_comp_size
# 0            S            60833.424
# 1            M           105914.964
# 2            S            60833.424

# 4            L            95483.400

sections.title: Using descriptive statistics and the IQR

steps.label: An outlier is an observation far away from other data points (e.g. a
steps.note:
An outlier is an observation far away from other data points (e.g. a
median house price of $400,000 vs. an outlier price of $5,000,000) —
worth investigating why, rather than assuming it's wrong.
steps.code:
print(salaries["Salary_USD"].describe())
# count       518.000
# mean     104905.826
# std       62660.107
# min        3819.000
# 25%       61191.000
# 50%       95483.000
# 75%      137496.000
# max      429675.000
# Name: Salary_USD, dtype: float64

steps.label: Interquartile range (IQR) = 75th percentile - 25th percentile.
steps.note:
Interquartile range (IQR) = 75th percentile - 25th percentile.
A boxplot visualizes the IQR directly (the box) along with outliers
(the points beyond the whiskers).
steps.code:
sns.boxplot(data=salaries,
            y="Salary_USD")
plt.show()

steps.label: Outlier rule of thumb based on the IQR:
steps.note:
Outlier rule of thumb based on the IQR:
Upper outliers: value > 75th percentile + (1.5 * IQR)
Lower outliers: value < 25th percentile - (1.5 * IQR)

steps.label: 75th percentile
steps.note: 75th percentile
steps.code:

syntaxPointers.label: List literal
syntaxPointers.explanation: Square brackets build a mutable list. Assigning a list to a new name shares the same object — mutating one view mutates both.

syntaxPointers.label: Tuple packing / unpacking
syntaxPointers.explanation: a, b = ... unpacks an iterable into multiple names by position. The right side must have exactly as many items as names.

syntaxPointers.label: Slicing [start:stop]
syntaxPointers.explanation: start is inclusive, stop is exclusive. Omitting either end defaults to the beginning/end; slicing never raises for out-of-range bounds.

syntaxPointers.label: for loop
syntaxPointers.explanation: Python's for iterates over items directly, not indices. To get indices too, wrap the iterable in enumerate().

syntaxPointers.label: Keyword argument at call site
syntaxPointers.explanation: name=value at a call passes by name, so order doesn't matter and intent is explicit. Keyword args must follow positional ones.

syntaxPointers.label: Lambda expression
syntaxPointers.explanation: lambda is a one-expression anonymous function. It can't contain statements — use a def when you need more than a single expression.

syntaxPointers.label: print()
syntaxPointers.explanation: print() writes to stdout with a trailing newline. Control it with sep=, end=, and file= — e.g. end='' to suppress the newline.

syntaxPointers.label: len()
syntaxPointers.explanation: len() is O(1) — containers store their size. It calls the object's __len__; objects without one raise TypeError.

syntaxPointers.label: String strip/replace
syntaxPointers.explanation: Strings are immutable, so these return a new string — the original is unchanged. Assign the result or it's lost.

syntaxPointers.label: map() / filter()
syntaxPointers.explanation: Both return lazy iterators, not lists — wrap in list() to materialize. A comprehension is usually clearer for the same job.

syntaxPointers.label: sum() / min() / max()
syntaxPointers.explanation: These consume any iterable. min/max accept a key= like sorted; on an empty iterable they raise unless you pass default=.

syntaxPointers.label: Double-bracket column selection
syntaxPointers.explanation: df[['a','b']] returns a DataFrame of several columns; the inner list is the column names. Single brackets df['a'] give a 1-D Series instead — the extra brackets are what keep it 2-D.

syntaxPointers.label: DataFrame .groupby()
syntaxPointers.explanation: groupby splits rows by key, then you apply an aggregation — nothing is computed until that aggregation runs (split-apply-combine).

syntaxPointers.label: DataFrame aggregation (.mean/.sum/.agg)
syntaxPointers.explanation: These skip NaN by default, so a column's mean ignores missing values silently — check .isna().sum() if that matters.

syntaxPointers.label: Method chaining
syntaxPointers.explanation: Chaining a.b().c() reads left-to-right as a pipeline. Each call returns an object the next call operates on; a broken link mid-chain is harder to debug.

quiz.question: Why must `df['col'].str.replace('a','')` be assigned back to the column?
options: str methods are slow, so caching speeds them up
String operations return a NEW Series; without reassignment the change is discarded
replace() mutates in place; the assignment is only for clarity
The assignment triggers a dtype recompute
quiz.explanation: Strings (and the Series wrapping them) are immutable — `.str.replace` returns a new Series. Not assigning it back leaves the original unchanged.

topics.title: Categorical Data Considerations, Feature Generation & Hypotheses
topics.description: Working with a flight-price ("planes") dataset to reason about whether a sample is representative, cross-tabulate categorical variables, engineer new numerical/categorical features (cleaned stop counts, date/time parts, price bands), and use correlation and grouped plots to generate — not yet test — hypotheses about what drives ticket price. Closes with a recap of the whole course.

toc.label: Why perform EDA, and representative data
Class frequency: value_counts() and relative frequency
Generating hypotheses: spurious correlation and data snooping

sections.title: Why perform EDA, and representative data

steps.label: EDA is useful for detecting patterns and relationships, generating
steps.note:
EDA is useful for detecting patterns and relationships, generating
questions/hypotheses, and preparing data for machine learning.

A sample is only useful if it's representative of the population you
care about — e.g. studying education vs. income in the USA can't rely
on data collected only in France.

steps.label: Categorical classes are just labels, e.g. marital status:
steps.note:
Categorical classes are just labels, e.g. marital status:
Single, Married, Divorced
Class imbalance is when some classes are far more frequent than others
in the sample.

sections.title: Class frequency: value_counts() and relative frequency

steps.label: .value_counts() shows the raw count of each class.
steps.note: .value_counts() shows the raw count of each class.

print(planes["Destination"].value_counts())

# Banglore     2773

steps.label: normalize=True converts counts to a proportion of the whole, e.g. 40% of
steps.note:

what's known about the population checks representativeness.

steps.label: The techniques covered across all four chapters, in one place:

syntaxPointers.label: List literal
syntaxPointers.explanation: Square brackets build a mutable list. Assigning a list to a new name shares the same object — mutating one view mutates both.

syntaxPointers.label: Keyword argument at call site
syntaxPointers.explanation: name=value at a call passes by name, so order doesn't matter and intent is explicit. Keyword args must follow positional ones.

syntaxPointers.label: print()
syntaxPointers.explanation: print() writes to stdout with a trailing newline. Control it with sep=, end=, and file= — e.g. end='' to suppress the newline.

syntaxPointers.label: String strip/replace
syntaxPointers.explanation: Strings are immutable, so these return a new string — the original is unchanged. Assign the result or it's lost.

syntaxPointers.label: sum() / min() / max()
syntaxPointers.explanation: These consume any iterable. min/max accept a key= like sorted; on an empty iterable they raise unless you pass default=.

syntaxPointers.label: Double-bracket column selection
syntaxPointers.explanation: df[['a','b']] returns a DataFrame of several columns; the inner list is the column names. Single brackets df['a'] give a 1-D Series instead — the extra brackets are what keep it 2-D.

syntaxPointers.label: DataFrame aggregation (.mean/.sum/.agg)
syntaxPointers.explanation: These skip NaN by default, so a column's mean ignores missing values silently — check .isna().sum() if that matters.

topics.title: What is Statistics? — Summary Statistics
topics.description: An introduction to what the field of statistics can (and can't) answer, the distinction between descriptive and inferential statistics, the main types of data, and how to summarize a dataset's center (mean, median, mode) and spread (variance, standard deviation, quantiles, IQR, outliers) using NumPy, pandas, and SciPy on the `msleep` mammal-sleep dataset (83 mammals' sleep statistics, assumed already loaded as a pandas DataFrame).

toc.label: Types of statistics: descriptive vs. inferential
Types of data: numeric vs. categorical
Why data type matters: summary statistics differ by type
How outliers affect mean vs. median
Using the IQR to find outliers
All summary statistics at once: describe()

steps.label: The field of statistics is the practice and study of collecting and
steps.note:
The field of statistics is the practice and study of collecting and
analyzing data. A summary statistic is a single fact/number that
summarizes a larger set of data.
Statistics can help answer questions such as:
- How likely is someone to purchase a product?
- How many occupants will a hotel have, and how can occupancy be optimized?
- What sizes of jeans need to be manufactured to fit 95% of the population?
- Which of two ads is more effective (A/B testing)?
What statistics can't do: it can't answer "why" questions directly.
e.g. "Why is Game of Thrones so popular?" can't be answered by statistics,
but a related, measurable question can be: "Are series with more violent
scenes viewed by more people?" Even then, correlation between violence and
views can't tell us that violent scenes *cause* more views.

sections.title: Types of statistics: descriptive vs. inferential

steps.label: Descriptive statistics: describe and summarize the data you have.
steps.note:
Descriptive statistics: describe and summarize the data you have.
e.g. 50% of friends drive to work, 25% take the bus, 25% bike.
Inferential statistics: use a sample of data to make inferences about a
larger population.
e.g. What percent of *all* people drive to work, based on a sample?

sections.title: Types of data: numeric vs. categorical

steps.label: Numeric (quantitative) data:
steps.note:
Numeric (quantitative) data:
Continuous (measured) - e.g. airplane speed, time spent waiting in line
Discrete (counted)    - e.g. number of pets, number of packages shipped
Categorical (qualitative) data:
Nominal (unordered)   - e.g. married/unmarried, country of residence
Ordinal (ordered)     - e.g. strongly disagree ... strongly agree
Categorical data can be represented as numbers, e.g.:
Nominal:  married/unmarried -> (1 / 0), country of residence -> (1, 2, ...)
Ordinal:  strongly disagree (1), somewhat disagree (2),
neither agree nor disagree (3), somewhat agree (4),
strongly agree (5)
Even when represented as numbers, these are still categorical: they can't
be meaningfully added, averaged, etc.

steps.note:

steps.note:

steps.note: 2. Square each distance.

steps.note: 3. Sum the squared distances.

sum_sq_dists = np.sum(sq_dists)

steps.note: 4. Divide by the number of data points minus 1.

steps.note:

the mean, instead of squaring it.

steps.note:

sections.title: Using the IQR to find outliers

steps.label: An outlier is a data point substantially different from the others. One
steps.note:
An outlier is a data point substantially different from the others. One
common rule: a data point is an outlier if
data < Q1 - 1.5 * IQR   or   data > Q3 + 1.5 * IQR
Apply the rule to bodywt.
steps.code:
bodywt_iqr = iqr(msleep['bodywt'])
lower_threshold = np.quantile(msleep['bodywt'], 0.25) - 1.5 * bodywt_iqr
upper_threshold = np.quantile(msleep['bodywt'], 0.75) + 1.5 * bodywt_iqr
print(msleep[(msleep['bodywt'] < lower_threshold) | (msleep['bodywt'] > upper_threshold)])
#                     name   vore  sleep_total    bodywt
# 4                    Cow  herbi          4.0   600.000

steps.label: A probability distribution describes the probability of each possible
steps.note:
A probability distribution describes the probability of each possible
outcome in a scenario.
The expected value is the mean of a probability distribution.
Expected value of a fair die roll:
(1 * 1/6) + (2 * 1/6) + (3 * 1/6) + (4 * 1/6) + (5 * 1/6) + (6 * 1/6) = 3.5
Expected value of an uneven die roll (where rolling a 2 is impossible):
(1 * 1/6) + (2 * 0) + (3 * 1/3) + (4 * 1/6) + (5 * 1/6) + (6 * 1/6) = 3.67

sections.title: The Poisson distribution: lambda and pmf/cdf

steps.label: A Poisson process: events appear to happen at a certain rate, but
steps.note:
A Poisson process: events appear to happen at a certain rate, but
completely at random, e.g. number of animals adopted from a shelter per
week, number of people arriving at a restaurant per hour, number of
earthquakes in California per year.
The time unit is irrelevant, as long as the same unit is used
consistently for the same situation.
The Poisson distribution describes the probability of some number of
events occurring over a fixed period of time, e.g. probability of >= 5
animals adopted per week, or < 20 earthquakes per year.
lambda (the distribution's peak) = average number of events per time
interval, e.g. average number of adoptions per week = 8.

steps.label: The exponential distribution describes the probability of a certain
steps.note:
The exponential distribution describes the probability of a certain
amount of time passing BETWEEN Poisson events, e.g. probability of > 1
day between adoptions, < 10 minutes between restaurant arrivals, or 6-8
months between earthquakes. It also uses lambda (rate), and is
continuous (time-based).
Example: on average, one customer service ticket is created every 2
minutes -> lambda = 0.5 tickets created each minute.
Expected value of the exponential distribution = 1 / lambda.
In terms of rate (Poisson): lambda = 0.5 requests per minute.
In terms of time between events (exponential): 1 / lambda = 1 / 0.5 = 2,
i.e. 1 request per 2 minutes.

syntaxPointers.label: List literal
syntaxPointers.explanation: Square brackets build a mutable list. Assigning a list to a new name shares the same object — mutating one view mutates both.

syntaxPointers.label: for loop
syntaxPointers.explanation: Python's for iterates over items directly, not indices. To get indices too, wrap the iterable in enumerate().

syntaxPointers.label: range()
syntaxPointers.explanation: range is a lazy sequence, not a list — it computes values on demand. range(stop) excludes stop, matching zero-based indexing.

syntaxPointers.label: Keyword argument at call site
syntaxPointers.explanation: name=value at a call passes by name, so order doesn't matter and intent is explicit. Keyword args must follow positional ones.

syntaxPointers.label: from ... import
syntaxPointers.explanation: from x import y pulls a name into the local namespace, so no prefix is needed — but it obscures which module y actually came from.

syntaxPointers.label: print()
syntaxPointers.explanation: print() writes to stdout with a trailing newline. Control it with sep=, end=, and file= — e.g. end='' to suppress the newline.

syntaxPointers.label: list .append() / .extend()
syntaxPointers.explanation: append adds one item; extend adds each item of an iterable. append(list) nests a whole list as a single element — a common mix-up.

syntaxPointers.label: DataFrame aggregation (.mean/.sum/.agg)
syntaxPointers.explanation: These skip NaN by default, so a column's mean ignores missing values silently — check .isna().sum() if that matters.

quiz.question: Why does averaging many samples produce a normal-looking distribution even from skewed data?
options: The Central Limit Theorem: means of many samples tend toward normal

Larger samples delete outliers automatically
It's a histogram-binning artifact
quiz.explanation: The CLT says the distribution of sample means approaches normal as the number of samples grows, even for skewed populations — which is why the mean is so well-behaved.

topics.description: How the correlation coefficient quantifies the strength and direction of a linear relationship between two variables, why it should never be trusted blindly (non-linear relationships, the need for data transformations, and the correlation-does-not-imply-causation trap with confounding variables), and the vocabulary of experimental design: controlled experiments, randomized controlled trials, placebos, double-blind trials, and observational (longitudinal vs. cross-sectional) studies.

sections.title: Relationships between two variables: x and y

steps.label: x = the explanatory/independent variable
steps.note:
x = the explanatory/independent variable
y = the response/dependent variable

steps.label: The correlation coefficient quantifies the linear relationship between
steps.note:
The correlation coefficient quantifies the linear relationship between
two variables. It's a number between -1 and 1:
- Magnitude corresponds to the strength of the relationship.
- Sign (+ or -) corresponds to the direction of the relationship.

steps.label: 0.99 -> very strong relationship
steps.note:
0.99 -> very strong relationship
0.75 -> strong relationship
0.56 -> moderate relationship
0.21 -> weak relationship
0.04 -> no relationship (knowing x tells us nothing about y)

steps.label: 0.75 -> as x increases, y increases
steps.note:
0.75 -> as x increases, y increases
-0.75 -> as x increases, y decreases

steps.label: .corr() is symmetric: the correlation of x with y is the same as y
steps.note:

print(msleep['sleep_rem'].corr(msleep['sleep_total']))   # 0.751755

steps.label: There are many ways to calculate correlation; this course uses the
steps.note:
There are many ways to calculate correlation; this course uses the
Pearson product-moment correlation (r), the most common measure:

r = (1 / (n - 1)) * sum_i[ (x_i - x_bar) * (y_i - y_bar) ] / (sigma_x * sigma_y)

where x_bar is the mean of x and sigma_x is the standard deviation of x
(similarly for y).
Variations on this formula include Kendall's tau and Spearman's rho.

steps.label: A relationship can look strongly patterned to the eye (e.g. a curve)
steps.note:
A relationship can look strongly patterned to the eye (e.g. a curve)
while still having a low correlation coefficient, because correlation
only measures LINEAR relationships.
r = 0.18

steps.note:

relationship more linear, increasing the correlation coefficient.

steps.label: Other common transformations:
steps.note:
Other common transformations:
- Log transformation:        log(x)
- Square root transformation: sqrt(x)
- Reciprocal transformation:  1 / x
Transformations can also be combined, e.g. log(x) and log(y), or
sqrt(x) and 1 / y.

steps.note:

- Linear regression (see: Introduction to Linear Modeling in Python)

sections.title: Correlation does not imply causation: confounding

steps.label: x being correlated with y does not mean x causes y. A third, unmeasured
steps.note:
x being correlated with y does not mean x causes y. A third, unmeasured
variable — a confounder — can influence both x and y, creating an
association between them that isn't causal.

steps.label: An experiment aims to answer: what is the effect of the treatment on
steps.note:
An experiment aims to answer: what is the effect of the treatment on
the response?
- Treatment: the explanatory/independent variable
- Response: the response/dependent variable
e.g. What is the effect of an advertisement (treatment) on the number of
products purchased (response)?

steps.label: In a controlled experiment, participants are assigned by researchers to
steps.note:
In a controlled experiment, participants are assigned by researchers to
either a treatment group (sees the advertisement) or a control group
(does not).
The two groups should be comparable so that causation can be inferred.
If the groups are not comparable, this can lead to confounding (bias),
e.g.:
Treatment group average age: 25
Control group average age: 50
-> Age is a potential confounder.

steps.label: The gold standard of experiments uses a randomized controlled trial:
steps.note:
The gold standard of experiments uses a randomized controlled trial:
participants are assigned to the treatment/control group randomly, not
based on any other characteristic. Choosing randomly helps ensure the
groups are comparable.
A placebo resembles the treatment but has no effect, so participants
don't know which group they're in. In clinical trials, a sugar pill
ensures any observed effect is due to the drug itself, not the idea of
receiving treatment.

steps.label: In a double-blind trial, the person administering the treatment or
steps.note:
In a double-blind trial, the person administering the treatment or
running the study also doesn't know whether a given participant is
receiving the real treatment or a placebo.
This prevents bias in the response and/or analysis of results.
Fewer opportunities for bias = a more reliable conclusion about
causation.

steps.label: In an observational study, participants are NOT assigned randomly to
steps.note:
In an observational study, participants are NOT assigned randomly to
groups — they assign themselves, usually based on pre-existing
characteristics.
Many research questions aren't conducive to a controlled experiment: you
can't force someone to smoke, to have a disease, or to have had certain
past behavior.
Observational studies can only establish association, not causation:
effects can be confounded by whatever factors got people into the
control or treatment group in the first place. There are ways to
control for confounders to get more reliable conclusions about
association.

steps.label: Longitudinal study: participants are followed over a period of time to
steps.note:
Longitudinal study: participants are followed over a period of time to
examine the effect of a treatment on a response.
e.g. the effect of age on height is not confounded by generation.
More expensive, and results take longer.
Cross-sectional study: data on participants is collected from a single
snapshot in time.
e.g. the effect of age on height IS confounded by generation.
Cheaper, faster, and more convenient.

syntaxPointers.label: Keyword argument at call site
syntaxPointers.explanation: name=value at a call passes by name, so order doesn't matter and intent is explicit. Keyword args must follow positional ones.

syntaxPointers.label: print()
syntaxPointers.explanation: print() writes to stdout with a trailing newline. Control it with sep=, end=, and file= — e.g. end='' to suppress the newline.
